content.full_text:
June 18, 1976

Mr. Timothy G. Laddish
26 Claremont Crescent
Berkeley, California 94705

Dear Mr. Laddish:

This letter is in response to your inquiry of June 3, 1976, concerning campaign disclosure requirements of the Political Reform Act.

Based on the facts presented in your June 3, 1976 letter to the Berkeley Fair Campaign Practices Commission, I am unable to advise whether or not CENA is subject to the reporting requirements of the Act. Therefore, I must qualify my response to your questions. Copies of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18215 and 18225 defining "contribution" and "expenditure" referred to below are enclosed.

While you state that none of CENA's dues are used to support candidates, your statement that dues are used for many purposes other than taking positions on ballot measures suggests that a portion may be used for purposes of influencing the actions of voters for or against the qualification or passage of measures. Under Government Code Section 82013(a), a person or group of persons becomes a recipient committee with reporting obligations once contributions received amount to $500 or more in a calendar year. If CENA collected $500 or more during this calendar year "earmarked" for the making of contributions or expenditures, CENA is a recipient committee. As indicated in Section 18225, a payment is "earmarked" at the time of making a payment, the donor knows or has reason to know that a portion of his or her dues will be used to make contributions or expenditures for political purposes. Factors relevant to a determination that the donor has reason to know that part of the dues will be used for political purposes include the established practice of the organization and representations made when the payment is solicited. Therefore, although no special collection was made for its June 1976 newsletter, CENA qualifies as a committee if it has collected $500 or more this year in earmarked dues. On the other hand, if CENA never uses any portion of its dues for political purposes, or if $500 or more in earmarked dues have not been collected this year, CENA is not a recipient committee.

[illegible] A

[illegible] for SHAT

[illegible] of VINYOMIP [illegible]
[illegible] Choneste [illegible] of
[illegible] [illegible] [illegible]

[The rest of the document appears to be typewritten text that is too blurry or faded in this image to transcribe with confidence. The text appears to be several paragraphs of dense content, but the individual words are not legible enough to transcribe accurately without introducing numerous errors.]

Mr. Timothy G. Laddish                    -2-                    June 18, 1976


CENA may be an "expenditure" committee, however, under Government
Code Section 82013(b) if its expenditures for political purposes
total $500 or more than a year.  To determine whether the costs of
publishing and distributing the June 1976 CENA newsletter are
expenditures as defined in Section 18225, we must consider whether
the newsletter is a regularly published periodical.  You stated
that the costs incurred in sending the newsletter to non-members
of CENA was less than $500; yet, if the June 1976 newsletter were
a special edition, CENA would be an expenditure committee because
the entire publication and distribution costs, which exceed $500,
are expenditures.  Furthermore, even if the newsletter were
published on its regular schedule, any additional costs incurred
because the newsletter was substantially altered in style, size or
format would be expenditures included in determining whether the
$500 threshold has been reached.

If you would care to advise how much, if any, of CENA's dues have
been earmarked for political purposes this year, whether the
June 1976 newsletter was published on its regular schedule and
how much, if any, additional costs were incurred in the June 1976
issue in conjunction with measure "O," our staff will provide a
definite response to your question.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        NAN HAMBLETON
                                        Special Compliance Representative

C.P'

June 3, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Ca. 95804

Dear Members of the Commission:

Re: Request for Opinion on Duties Under
Political Reform Act of 1974

I am the treasurer of the Claremont-Elmwood Neighborhood
Association (hereinafter referred to as CENA), a nonprofit
corporation which was originally officially formed as an
unincorporated association in 1970. Enclosed is a copy of
the CENA newsletter which is currently being distributed. Also
enclosed is a copy of my letter to the Berkeley Fair Campaign
Practices Commission, which sets forth the pertinent facts
regarding CENA and this newsletter.

Under the facts as stated in the enclosed letter it
would appear that CENA has not brought itself within the
requirements of the Political Reform Act of 1974. However,
I request the opinion of the Commission as to what duties (if
any) CENA, and I as treasurer of CENA, might have under said
Act.

Thank you for your assistance. My address is as follows:

Timothy G. Laddish
26 Claremont Crescent
Berkeley, Ca. 94705

Very truly yours,

Timothy G. Laddish
Timothy G. Laddish

I can see this is a nearly blank document with only a few faint elements visible at the top of the page. The image appears to be mostly white/blank space with what looks like two small curved marks or symbols near the top, and possibly some very faint dots on the right side.

Since the document is essentially blank or the text is too faint to read reliably, I cannot provide a meaningful transcription of actual content.

June 3, 1976


Berkeley Fair Campaign Practices Commission
c/o Edythe Campbell, City Clerk
Berkeley City Hall
Berkeley, Ca. 94704


Dear Members of the Commission:

     Re: Request for Opinion on Duties Under
          Berkeley Election Reform Act of 1974

     I am the treasurer of the Claremont-Elmwood Neighborhood
Association (hereinafter referred to as CENA), a nonprofit
corporation, which was originally officially formed as an
unincorporated association in 1970.  Enclosed is a copy of the
CENA newsletter which is currently being distributed. The cost
of this newsletter is being paid from the CENA treasury, which
is financed by annual dues ($5.00 annually paid by CENA members. CENA dues ($5.00
consists of annual dues paid by CENA members. CENA dues ($5.00
per year) are used for many purposes other than taking positions
on measures pending before the electorate; no CENA dues are used
for supporting candidates for elective office. No special
collection or assessment was made to pay for this particular
newsletter, and CENA is not controlled, directly or indirectly,
by any other group.

     As can be seen from the newsletter, the newsletter (with
its gold insert) advocates a "no" vote on Berkeley Initiative
ordinance "O", a position consistent with CENA's past policies.
By this letter, I am requesting an opinion from the Commission on
whether CENA's expenditures regarding this newsletter make CENA
subject to the requirements of the Berkeley Election Reform Act
of 1974 as affected by recent court decisions.

     CENA has had 3000 of these newsletters (with the gold
inserts) printed, almost all with 500 extra copies of the gold insert.
Approximately 2600 of the newsletters have been mailed, with
the remainder (including the extra gold inserts) to be distributed
by hand. 536 of the 2600 newsletters were mailed to
CENA members. The costs have been as follows: $80.00 for
typesetting, $267.19 for printing, and approximately $200.00
for postage (at the bulk rate of 7.72 apiece). When the costs
attributable to providing the newsletter to CENA's membership
are deducted, less than $500.00 remains as expenditures attributable
to providing the newsletter to nonmembers. I understand that
other groups opposing measure "O" intend to print and distribute
further copies of the gold insert.

     1. One of the purposes specified in CENA's articles of
incorporation is to serve "as a focal point for neighborhood
concerns involving such issues as traffic and transit . . . ."

I can see this image is almost entirely blank or extremely faint. There are some very faint marks visible at the top of the page and possibly at the bottom, but they are too illegible for me to transcribe accurately.
